headline: Half a Million Wait Out the Siege of El Obeid
deck: The RSF has ringed North Kordofan's capital and pounded it with ten straight days of drone strikes; the UN warns of a coming catastrophe and calls El Obeid the likely next El Fasher. Whoever holds the city holds the gate between Darfur and central Sudan.

art.title: The Gate: Darfur to Central Sudan
art.caption: El Obeid, capital of North Kordofan, sits on the route between RSF-held Darfur and the army-held centre; the UN calls it the likely next site of mass atrocity after El Fasher · Map: Sprockett, Escalation Desk · Terrain: NOAA ETOPO1

body: Half a million people are trapped in El Obeid as the Rapid Support Forces tighten a ring around the North Kordofan capital, according to Al Jazeera's account of the siege [E1]. Civilians have endured siege-like conditions for eighteen months, and ten consecutive days of drone strikes have killed at least fifty people across the city and the surrounding state, damaging schools and displacement sites [E1]. Food prices have jumped as much as 300 percent and water prices have doubled, while the security situation has choked off aid [E1].

The city is a prize because of where it sits. El Obeid straddles a key route between the RSF-controlled Darfur region and the army-held centre and east, the gateway through which goods, people and supplies move into central Sudan [E1]. Control of that junction shapes who can resupply what, which is why the paramilitary has spent months encircling it rather than bypassing it [E1].

United Nations investigators have named the danger in advance. The Human Rights Council's fact-finding mission, which last week found that the RSF's earlier assault on El Fasher bore the markers of genocide, has warned that the same tactics are now trained on El Obeid and opened an urgent inquiry into the city [E2]. UN human-rights chief Volker Türk put the exposure plainly, saying "the risk of summary executions, abduction, arbitrary detention and other violence against civilians is high" [E3].

The comparison to El Fasher is the load-bearing fear. There, the RSF took a city after a long siege and carried out door-to-door killings that a UN mission and Amnesty International described in the language of mass atrocity [E2]. El Obeid now shows the same sequence, encirclement, infrastructure strikes, collapsing aid, that preceded the worst of Darfur [E1][E2].

The bounded reading matters. A siege is not yet an assault, and the record here does not show that a ground offensive has begun or that any single outcome is fixed [E1][E2]. Sieges have ended in negotiated withdrawals before, and the RSF has an incentive to avoid a second documented genocide finding [E2]. The discriminators are grimly concrete, whether aid corridors open, whether the drone campaign lifts, or whether the ground clashes the UN anticipates arrive. For half a million people, the gap between siege and assault is the whole of the news [E1][E3].

evidence_box.fragment: Half a million trapped in El Obeid; ten days of drone strikes killed at least 50; the city sits on a key route between RSF-held Darfur and army-held eastern regions; food up 300%, water doubled
Fact-finding mission found El Fasher atrocities bear markers of genocide and opened an urgent inquiry into El Obeid, warning the same tactics are now aimed at the city
Volker Türk: "the risk of summary executions, abduction, arbitrary detention and other violence against civilians is high" as RSF besiege El Obeid
